gloss: negation, non-being, absence — the foundational negative of Chinese philosophy, recruited by Chan as a pedagogical tool rather than an ontological claim
notes: Unlike Sanskrit śūnya, 無 is a native Chinese negative with no Sanskrit original. Chan teachers shaped its meaning through use, not translation.

gloss: emptiness — the absence of svabhāva (inherent, independent self-existence) in all phenomena
notes: Central term of Madhyamaka philosophy (Nāgārjuna). Things are empty not because they don't exist but because they exist only interdependently. The Chinese 空 carries this but flattens the relational logic.

gloss: citta: mind, consciousness, the thinking-feeling organ; hṛdaya: heart, the physical and spiritual center
notes: Chinese 心 conflates both Sanskrit terms into one, reflecting a Chinese conceptual unity that neither Sanskrit term alone captures.

gloss: mārga: path, road, the way to liberation; tao: the Way — the underlying ordering principle of reality in Daoist and Confucian thought
notes: Buddhist translators used the pre-existing Chinese 道 (tao) to render Sanskrit mārga, inheriting centuries of Daoist and Confucian resonance. Chan exploited this layering deliberately.

gloss: the love of wisdom; systematic rational inquiry into the nature of existence, knowledge, value, and mind
notes: Nishi Amane encountered the term primarily through Dutch philosophy during his studies in the Netherlands. His coinage frames Western philosophy through the lens of Confucian sage-wisdom.

gloss: an abstract collective of individuals bound by shared norms and institutions — distinct from the state, the family, and mere population
notes: A specifically modern Western sociological concept. Classical Chinese thought organized social reality through relational categories (the five relationships), not through an abstract collective sphere.

gloss: rule by the people; a system of government in which political authority derives from and is exercised by the citizenry
notes: The classical Chinese compound 民主 meant 'lord of the people' (paternalistic), not 'rule by the people.' Repurposing it for democracy inverts the classical political relationship.

gloss: awakening, enlightenment — the direct, unmediated seeing of reality as it is; the cognitive event that constitutes liberation
notes: From the Sanskrit root budh- (to awake, to know). Bodhi is not a belief or a doctrine but an event — the sudden or gradual opening of direct perception of the nature of mind and phenomena. Sambodhi (complete awakening) and anuttarā-samyak-sambodhi (unsurpassed, complete, perfect awakening) are standard formal terms. The Chinese 悟 renders the ordinary Buddhist usage of bodhi as the transformative recognition event, while 覚 (kaku) tends to carry more of the gradual-cultivation sense of Sanskrit bodhi as ongoing wakefulness.

gloss: intentional action — volitional deed that conditions the stream of consciousness and future experience
notes: From Sanskrit root kṛ- (to do, to make). In Buddhist usage, karma is not fate or cosmic justice but a psychological-causal claim: it is specifically cetanā (intention, volition) that constitutes karma, not the physical act alone. The three modes of karma — bodily (kāya-karman), verbal (vāk-karman), mental (mano-karman) — establish that thought itself is an action with karmic force. The Chinese 業 captures the deed/action sense precisely but loses the specific emphasis on intention as the defining factor; Buddhist commentators in both Chinese and Japanese traditions were careful to restore this qualification.

gloss: systematic empirical inquiry into the natural world; organized, methodical knowledge production
notes: The German Wissenschaft (systematic organized knowledge, including humanistic disciplines) fits the coinage 科学 better than English 'science,' which by the mid-19th century meant primarily natural science.

gloss: seeing one's nature — the direct perceptual breakthrough into Buddha-nature; the foundational Chan awakening event expressed as a transitive act of sight
notes: Not a translation from Sanskrit: 見性 is a Chan Chinese formulation crystallized in the Platform Sutra slogan 見性成仏 ('seeing one's nature, becoming Buddha'). The Sanskrit closest to it would be buddha-dhātu-darśana (seeing the Buddha-element) or ātma-darśana (seeing the self/nature), but Chan teachers developed the concept independently from the formulation 'what is one's nature?' as a meditation question. The critical doctrinal tension is whether 見 (seeing) implies a subject that sees and an object (性, nature) that is seen — Dōgen problematizes this in 'Busshō,' arguing that if Buddha-nature is all-encompassing, the subject-object grammar of 見性 must be dissolved.

gloss: informal assembly — a less formal dharma dialogue in which the teacher offers a short talk and holds exchange with attendees, contrasted with the grand formality of jōdō
notes: The complement of 上堂 in the Chan/Zen monastic schedule. Where jōdō is the fully formal convocation with the abbot ascending the high seat in the dharma hall, shōsan is a more relaxed, intimate gathering in which the teacher may speak briefly and then engage in extended question-and-answer. The format is typically held in a less formal space or without the full ceremonial apparatus of jōdō. In contemporary Western Zen practice, shōsan has been widely adopted as a public question-and-answer format, sometimes as an alternative to dokusan for practitioners who may not have a formal koan practice.

gloss: ascending the hall — the formal convocation in which the abbot mounts the high seat in the dharma hall and addresses the full assembly, sometimes engaging in spontaneous mondō
notes: A Chan/Zen monastic institution traceable to at least the Tang dynasty. The jōdō is one of the most formal ritual occasions in the Zen monastic calendar: the abbot ascends the high seat (高座, kōza) in the dharma hall (法堂, hattō), sometimes incenses, and delivers a formal address or engages in spontaneous question-and-answer with members of the assembly. The format is distinct from teishō (which is commentary on a specific koan or text) in that jōdō may be more spontaneous, more responsive to the occasion, and more interactive. The Song-dynasty recorded sayings collections (語録, goroku) preserve large numbers of jōdō addresses, making the form one of the primary literary genres of Chan/Zen.

gloss: going to Zen — private interview with a master emphasizing the student's initiative in approaching the teacher; functionally overlapping with dokusan but foregrounding the movement of approach rather than the privacy of the encounter
notes: Closely related to 独参 (dokusan) and sometimes used interchangeably. The distinction, when observed, is one of emphasis: dokusan foregrounds the private one-on-one character of the interview; sanzen foregrounds the student's initiative in going to the master. In institutional practice some lineages use sanzen as the primary term for the private interview during sesshin, while others reserve dokusan for the specific Rinzai format. The compound 参禅 can also function more broadly as 'going to [study] Zen' — the student's initial approach to a Zen master for training.

gloss: private one-on-one interview with a Zen master — the institutional form of the intimate encounter in which the student presents understanding and the master evaluates, the core mechanism of Rinzai koan training
notes: A Chan/Zen institutional form without a direct Sanskrit antecedent. Dokusan is the mechanism through which the koan curriculum is worked through: the student meditates on the koan, develops a response or a demonstration of understanding, and presents it to the master in a private interview. The master accepts, rejects, or redirects the student's presentation. The interview is confidential — what passes between master and student in dokusan is not shared with others — and the format is brief and concentrated, typically lasting only a few minutes. In Rinzai sesshin, dokusan is offered multiple times daily, and the rhythm of sitting-and-interview, sitting-and-interview creates the pressure that drives the koan practice forward.

gloss: formal dharma presentation — a master's commentary on a koan or Zen text, understood as a demonstration of realized insight rather than an explanation; the vocal form that Zen teaching takes in its most formal register
notes: A Chan/Zen institutional form with no direct Sanskrit antecedent. The compound 提唱 — to hold up and proclaim — names the master's public presentation of a koan or text as a demonstration of realized understanding. The teishō is not a lecture: it does not explain the koan's meaning in intellectual terms but enacts the insight the koan points at through the master's voice, timing, and presence. In the Rinzai tradition teishō is closely associated with the koan curriculum: the master presents a koan and its associated commentaries as a living demonstration. In the Sōtō tradition teishō has a broader range, sometimes covering doctrinal exposition as well as koan commentary. In both cases the distinction from ordinary lecturing is maintained: the audience is invited not to learn information but to receive the demonstration.

gloss: question-and-answer — the formal dialogic exchange between master and student; the mode underlying most koan records, in which the student's question is met by the master's response that refuses, shatters, or redirects the question's conceptual frame
notes: Not a translation from Sanskrit but a Chinese compound naming the dialogic form that Chan/Zen developed as its primary pedagogical vehicle. The koan literature — Gateless Gate, Blue Cliff Record, Book of Serenity — is almost entirely composed of recorded 問答 exchanges. The form is distinctive: unlike Socratic dialogue (which proceeds by elenchos toward a conclusion), Chan mondō typically turns on a single exchange in which the master's response cuts through the student's question rather than answering it. The student asks expecting an answer within the conceptual frame the question opens; the master's response refuses the frame entirely. The point is not the transmission of information but the occasion of direct insight — the student is meant to see through the question, not to receive an answer to it.

gloss: face-to-face transmission — the direct, intimate passing of dharma from master to disciple in physical co-presence; the mode of transmission Dōgen considered essential to authentic lineage
notes: A Chan compound given its most consequential articulation by Dōgen, who insisted that face-to-face transmission was the only legitimate mode of dharma succession. Dōgen's emphasis was partly polemical: against schools that accepted transmission through texts, dreams, or visionary encounters, he argued that the physical co-presence of master and disciple was non-negotiable. The Shōbōgenzō fascicle 'Menju' elaborates the claim in detail. The doctrinal stake is that what is transmitted in dharma succession is not information (which could be conveyed by text) but the awakened mind itself (which can only be recognized in the living encounter between two persons). This makes 面授 not merely an institutional preference but a doctrinal requirement: the structure of mind-to-mind transmission demands face-to-face encounter.

gloss: dharma transmission — formal recognition of a disciple as a dharma heir, continuing the lineage from Śākyamuni Buddha through the Indian and Chinese patriarchs to the present teacher
notes: A Chan/Zen institutional concept without a direct Sanskrit antecedent. Dharma transmission is the mechanism by which the Chan/Zen tradition maintains its self-understanding as an unbroken lineage from the historical Buddha. The transmission is not the passing of a body of doctrine but the master's formal recognition that the disciple has attained authentic realization and is qualified to teach in their own right. The institutional form varies between lineages: Rinzai transmission is typically associated with the completion of the formal koan curriculum; Sōtō transmission involves a formal ceremony of document-copying and acknowledgment. Dōgen placed extreme emphasis on 面授 (menju, face-to-face transmission), insisting that authentic lineage requires the physical co-presence of master and disciple.

gloss: the mind of awakening — the aspiration to attain complete Buddhahood for the benefit of all sentient beings; the essential first step of the Mahāyāna path
notes: From Sanskrit bodhi (awakening) + citta (mind). Bodhicitta is the aspiration that distinguishes the Mahāyāna path from the Hīnayāna: the practitioner seeks awakening not for personal liberation alone but for the benefit of all sentient beings. East Asian Buddhism renders the concept as 菩提心 (bodaishin, the awakening-mind), and the compound 発菩提心 (hotsu bodaishin, arousing the awakening-mind) names the first moment at which this aspiration arises. Dōgen treats hotsu bodaishin as the essential foundation of the Buddhist path in the Shōbōgenzō fascicle of the same name, emphasizing that the aspiration is not a feeling or a resolve but the first functional expression of the practice-realization unity: the aspiration to awaken is already itself an expression of the awakening it seeks.

gloss: negotiating the Way — wholehearted engagement in practice; the active, full-bodied exertion of the practitioner in the work of the Buddhist path
notes: The compound 弁道 appears in earlier Chan vocabulary but takes its most consequential articulation in Dōgen's Bendōwa (弁道話, 'A Talk on Negotiating the Way'), the foundational essay of his teaching career. The 弁 is doing precise work: it names practice not as following instructions or performing a technique but as actively negotiating — engaging with, working through, and throwing oneself into the Way. The word 'negotiate' is closer to the original sense than 'practice' alone: the Way is not something one merely follows but something one engages with as a counterpart, something that responds to the practitioner's exertion in ways that cannot be fully predicted. The essay Bendōwa is the primary text in which Dōgen introduces his major doctrinal categories: 修証一等, 自受用三昧, the universality of zazen, the superiority of shikantaza.

gloss: continuous practice — the sustained, ceaseless activity of the Buddhist Way; practice conceived not as episodic effort but as an unbroken circle that sustains itself across moments, lives, and the generations of the lineage
notes: A Chinese compound reactivated with new force in Dōgen's Shōbōgenzō, where it is the subject of a major two-part fascicle ('Gyōji'). The term is present in earlier Chan literature but Dōgen gives it a distinctive reading: 行持 names not the practitioner's effort to practice continuously but the Way's own continuous activity expressed through the practitioner. The practice sustains itself — the practitioner participates in rather than generates the continuity. This reading supports the related concept of 行持道環 (gyōji dōkan, the circle of the Way in continuous practice), in which the practice-realization relationship is figured as a self-sustaining circle rather than a linear path from practice to result.

gloss: self-receiving and self-employing samādhi — Dōgen's term from the Bendōwa for zazen as a meditative absorption that receives itself from itself and employs itself in itself, requiring no external occasion or further purpose to be complete
notes: A signature Dōgen formulation built from the Sanskrit-derived 三昧 (samādhi) and the philosophical pair 自受 (self-receiving) and 自用 (self-employing). The compound describes zazen as a samādhi whose source, content, and purpose are all internal to the practice itself: the practice receives itself from itself, employs itself in itself, and serves no further goal because it is already the activity any further goal would name. The doctrinal stake is the same as 修証一等 (oneness of practice and realization): if practice is the activity of awakening rather than a means to it, then the samādhi of practice cannot be received from outside or employed for some further purpose, because there is no outside and no further purpose. The Bendōwa essay introduces the term and elaborates its significance.

gloss: the rain retreat — the intensive communal training period of three months traditionally observed during the South Asian monsoon, in which monks ceased traveling and gathered for sustained practice; the East Asian and Japanese descendants of this institution
notes: The Sanskrit varṣā (rain) names the original institutional context: during the Indian monsoon (roughly June to September) monks could not travel without damaging crops and ground-dwelling life, so they gathered at fixed locations for sustained practice. This pragmatic accommodation became one of the most stable institutions of Buddhist monasticism and was carried throughout the Buddhist world. East Asian Buddhism preserved the institution as 安居 (Chinese ānjū, Japanese ango), though the seasonal alignment shifted to fit local climate — Japanese ango is often held in summer (夏安居) but also exists as a winter retreat (雪安居). In Chan/Zen monasteries the period is the principal occasion for sustained communal practice and ceremonial markers like the appointment of the shuso (head student) and the hossenshiki (dharma combat ceremony) at its conclusion.

gloss: meditative absorption — the unified, one-pointed concentration in which mind and object converge into a single field of awareness; one of the foundational categories of Buddhist meditation psychology
notes: From the Sanskrit roots sam- (together) and ā-dhā (to place), literally the placing-together that constitutes unified attention. Samādhi is one of the three pillars of the Buddhist path in early formulations (śīla, samādhi, prajñā — ethics, concentration, wisdom) and is analyzed in detail in Abhidharma psychology as a class of meditative attainments with internal taxonomy. East Asian Buddhism inherits the Sanskrit term phonetically as 三昧 (Chinese sānmèi, Japanese zanmai) and uses the compound for both the technical concentration of meditation and the broader sense of complete absorption in any activity. In Chan/Zen contexts, samādhi is understood not as a special state to be achieved but as the natural mode of awareness that becomes available when grasping ceases — closer to the Sōtō understanding of zazen as the activity of awakening than to the gradualist conception of samādhi as a stage on a progressive path.

gloss: gathering the mind — an intensive meditation retreat, typically five to seven days, of near-continuous zazen with all monastic activities organized to support the singleness of practice
notes: A specifically Chan/Zen monastic institution rather than a translation from Sanskrit. Sesshin retreats developed within the Chan tradition as periods of concentrated practice during which the regular monastic schedule intensifies: zazen sessions extend, walking meditation, formal meals, and brief work periods are tightly scheduled, sleep is reduced, and casual conversation is suspended. The structure is designed so that all the conditions of monastic life converge to support the gathering of the mind into the unified attention that practice requires. In contemporary Japanese and Western Zen practice, sesshin remains the principal occasion for sustained intensive practice, typically held several times a year in established monasteries and Zen centers.

gloss: walking meditation — slow, mindful walking practiced between periods of seated zazen, in which the activity of walking is engaged with the same quality of attention as the activity of sitting
notes: The Sanskrit antecedent is caṅkrama, the walking meditation practiced in the early Buddhist forest tradition and described in the Pali canon. The Chinese 経行 (jīngxíng) preserves the practice in monastic East Asian Buddhism and is integral to Chan/Zen monastic schedules: between periods of zazen, monks walk slowly in single file around the meditation hall or along a designated path, maintaining the same quality of embodied attention they bring to sitting. The doctrinal point is that practice is not confined to the seated posture — the activity of walking, engaged with the same quality of presence, is itself the practice. This is consistent with the Sōtō principle that practice is the activity of awakening rather than a means to it.

gloss: just sitting — Dōgen's name for objectless, technique-free meditation in which the activity of sitting is itself the entire practice, with no further goal, no koan, no breath count, and no aim beyond the sitting that is happening
notes: A specifically Sōtō term, formed from the Chinese 只管 (single-mindedly, exclusively) and 打坐 (doing sitting). Dōgen learned the practice from his Chinese teacher Tiantong Rujing (Tendō Nyojō) at Mt. Tendō and made it the central practice of the Sōtō tradition he founded in Japan. The doctrinal point of shikantaza is the refusal of any framework that would treat zazen as a technique aimed at producing some other state. There is nothing to achieve through sitting because sitting is itself the activity of awakening (修証一等, oneness of practice and realization). The practice is therefore not the application of a method but the dropping of all methods, and what remains is simply the sitting that practice consists in. Shikantaza is often misread as 'sitting and doing nothing' or as 'mindfulness of sitting,' both of which import precisely the goal-orientation it refuses.

gloss: meditative absorption — the focused concentration of mind in sustained meditation; the practice of structured contemplation that all Buddhist traditions inherit from the pre-Buddhist śramaṇa milieu
notes: The Sanskrit dhyāna names a specific class of meditative attainments analyzed in detail in early Buddhist psychology — the four rūpa-jhānas of form-meditation and the four arūpa-jhānas of formless meditation. East Asian Buddhism inherited this analysis but the technical taxonomy is rarely the foreground of Chan/Zen practice; the term 禅 became attached to the school precisely because the school took meditation as its defining activity rather than as one technical category among others. The phonetic transliteration 禅 (chán → zen) preserves the Sanskrit sound while abandoning most of the technical taxonomic baggage, which is doctrinally fitting: Chan/Zen is interested in dhyāna as the activity through which awakening is occasioned, not as a graduated map of meditative states to be achieved sequentially.

gloss: impermanence-is-Buddha-nature — Dōgen's radical formulation in the Shōbōgenzō 'Busshō' fascicle, identifying impermanence (the foundational mark of all conditioned phenomena) with Buddha-nature itself, dissolving the apparent opposition between the Three Dharma Seals and Tathāgatagarbha doctrine
notes: Not a translation from Sanskrit but a Dōgen formulation built from existing Buddhist vocabulary. The doctrinal stake is significant. The Three Dharma Seals identify all conditioned things as impermanent (諸行無常); Tathāgatagarbha and certain Mahāyāna sutras affirm Buddha-nature as constant (常). These two claims have generated centuries of doctrinal exegesis trying to reconcile them. Dōgen's move in the 'Busshō' fascicle is to refuse the reconciliation by collapsing the opposition: impermanence is not the obstacle to Buddha-nature but is Buddha-nature, and the constancy attributed to Buddha-nature must be understood not as the absence of change but as the very mode in which change occurs. The formulation is philosophically dense and has been the subject of extensive scholarship in modern Sōtō studies.

gloss: original mind — the inherently pure, undefiled mind prior to the arising of delusion; in Chan/Zen vocabulary the mode of mind that is to be recognized rather than achieved
notes: A Chinese-Japanese formulation rather than a translation of a single Sanskrit term. The compound 本心 carries the structural sense of 本 (root, origin, originally) applied to 心 (heart-mind): the mind in its original condition, prior to and beneath the elaborations of delusion. The Platform Sutra uses the term centrally — 不識本心、学法無益 (not recognizing the original mind, learning the dharma is useless) — as the foundational claim of Chan/Zen practice. The doctrinal point is that the original mind is not a memory of a former state or a reachable future state but the present mind seen as it actually is, prior to the constructions that ordinarily cover it over.

gloss: Buddha-mind — the awakened mind, equivalent in function to Buddha-nature but emphasizing the cognitive-affective register; the mind of an awakened being, identified by Chan/Zen with the practitioner's own mind when free of obstruction
notes: The compound 仏心 (Chinese fóxīn, Japanese busshin) translates and elaborates the Sanskrit buddha-citta in a register that emphasizes the heart-mind aspect of awakening over the abstract notion of Buddha-nature (仏性). The Chan/Zen tradition is sometimes called 仏心宗 (busshinshū, the Buddha-mind school), reflecting the school's foundational claim that what is transmitted from master to disciple is not a doctrine but the awakened mind itself, recognized as already present in the disciple. The compound foregrounds the same identification that 即心是仏 makes more concentrated: there is no Buddha apart from the mind, and no mind apart from Buddha when ordinary discriminating activity is not generating the appearance of separation.

term: píngcháng xīn shì dào / byōjōshin kore dō
gloss: ordinary mind is the Way — Mazu Daoyi's formulation that the everyday, unexceptional functioning of mind is itself the Way of practice and awakening, not a stepping stone toward something more exalted
notes: Attributed to Mazu Daoyi, the same Tang Chan master associated with 即心是仏. The formula appears most famously in Mumonkan Case 19, where Nansen (Nanquan Puyuan) gives it to his student Jōshū (Zhaozhou Congshen) — though the formulation is older and is associated with the broader Hongzhou school. The doctrinal point is to refuse the gradualist temptation to treat practice as the cultivation of an extraordinary mind that does not yet exist. The mind that practice engages is the ordinary mind one already has — the only mind there is — and the Way is what that ordinary mind does when it is not generating obstructions. The formula is often misread as a quietist license to do nothing special; the koan record is consistently careful to refuse this reading.

term: jí xīn shì fó / sokushin zebutsu
gloss: this very mind is Buddha — Mazu Daoyi's cardinal Chan formula asserting that the presently functioning mind is itself Buddhahood, with no gap, no mediation, and no future state to be reached
notes: Attributed to Mazu Daoyi (709–788), the Tang-dynasty Chan master whose teaching shaped the Hongzhou school and through it the entire later Chan/Zen tradition. The formula appears in the Mumonkan (Gateless Gate) Case 30 and in the Blue Cliff Record Case 73. Mazu's biography records that he later modified the formula to 非心非仏 (not mind, not Buddha) when he saw students clinging to 即心是仏 as a doctrinal position to be held — the modification preserves the doctrinal point while breaking the attachment to its formulation. The two formulas together model the characteristic Chan refusal to let any teaching become a fixed position the practitioner can rest in.

gloss: body-mind as one — the doctrinal claim that the lived body and the heart-mind are not two separable entities but a single field of embodied experience, foundational to Dōgen's account of zazen as the activity of the whole person
notes: Not directly a translation from Sanskrit. The phrase crystallizes a position with deep roots in Mahāyāna nondualism but takes its specifically Japanese articulation in Dōgen's Shōbōgenzō, where the unity of body and mind is repeatedly affirmed against any framing that would treat practice as a mental discipline conducted in a separable body. The doctrinal stake is significant: if body and mind are one, then zazen is not the mind sitting in a body but the whole embodied person engaging in a single integrated activity, and the gradualist temptation to treat the body as the obstacle to mental clarity is dissolved at its root.

gloss: own-nature, inherent self-existence — the doctrinally contested notion that a thing has an independent, intrinsic essence that constitutes it apart from its causes and relations
notes: The central target of Madhyamaka critique. Nāgārjuna's Mūlamadhyamakakārikā argues at length that nothing has svabhāva, because everything arises dependently on causes and conditions, and what arises dependently cannot also exist independently. The Chinese rendering 自性 preserves the structure: 自 (self) + 性 (nature). The doctrinal tension is that East Asian Tathāgatagarbha-influenced traditions sometimes use 自性 positively — to refer to one's original Buddha-nature as 'self-nature' — creating an apparent collision with Madhyamaka's denial. Careful Chan/Zen commentators distinguish two senses: the svabhāva that Madhyamaka denies (inherent independent essence) and the 自性 that Chan affirms (the nature of mind that is to be seen, which is itself empty of svabhāva in the Madhyamaka sense). The terminological collision is real and has generated centuries of doctrinal exegesis.

gloss: the nature of dharmas — the intrinsic, defining character of phenomena; what dharmas are in themselves prior to any conceptual or practical sorting
notes: An abstract noun formed from dharma, parallel in formation to tathatā (suchness) from tathā (thus). Dharmatā names the nature of phenomena considered as a whole — what they share by virtue of being phenomena at all. In Madhyamaka the answer to 'what is the nature of dharmas?' is emptiness (śūnyatā): dharmas have no inherent self-nature, and that absence is their dharmatā. The Chinese rendering 法性 (fǎxìng, hosshō) preserves the abstract structure but lets later doctrinal traditions read it in different ways — sometimes as the bare absence Madhyamaka points at, sometimes as a positive ground in Tathāgatagarbha-influenced readings. Dōgen devotes a Shōbōgenzō fascicle to 法性, treating it as a dynamic and constantly self-manifesting reality rather than a static metaphysical substrate.

gloss: suchness, thusness — the true nature of reality just as it is, prior to and beyond all conceptual elaboration; the way things are when not overlaid by the discriminating activity of mind
notes: Central term of Yogācāra and Madhyamaka thought, derived from the simple Sanskrit demonstrative tathā ('thus, in this way'). Tathatā names not a special metaphysical substance but the bare fact of things being as they are — a deflationary rather than inflationary concept. The Awakening of Faith treatise treats tathatā as the absolute ground; later East Asian elaborations sometimes hypostatize it as a metaphysical reality, against which the Madhyamaka strain of the tradition consistently warns. The Chinese rendering 真如 doubles the emphasis with 真 (true) — the true thusness, as opposed to a mistaken or partial one. The compound is doctrinally inseparable from the epithet 如来 (Tathāgata, 'thus-come'), which makes the same word the name of a Buddha.

gloss: no-mind — awareness free from fixation, clinging, and the discriminating self-reference of the ordinary ego; the unobstructed functioning of consciousness when it is not grasping at its own contents
notes: A Chan Chinese formulation rather than a translation from Sanskrit, though it has affinities with the Sanskrit acitta and with the Mādhyamika critique of clinging. The term enters Chan vocabulary by the Tang period and becomes central to the Linji/Rinzai pedagogical tradition. The 'no' of 無心 does not mean the absence of consciousness — that would be 不覚 (un-wakefulness) or simple unconsciousness — but the absence of the grasping, fixating activity by which the ordinary mind constructs an objectified self separate from its experience. 無心 as a positive term names the unobstructed flow that is uncovered when this construction stops. The term has substantial life outside Buddhist texts as well: in Japanese aesthetics and the martial arts traditions of the Tokugawa period (kendō, kyūdō, sadō, shodō), 無心 names the state of skilled action in which technique has become so internalized that conscious deliberation no longer interferes.

gloss: Buddha-nature; the Buddha-element or womb of the Tathāgata — the doctrine that all sentient beings possess (or are) the intrinsic nature of awakening, the seed or substrate of Buddhahood
notes: Two distinct but overlapping Sanskrit terms — buddha-dhātu (the Buddha-element) and tathāgatagarbha (the womb or embryo of the Tathāgata) — converge in the East Asian rendering 仏性 (Chinese fóxìng, Japanese busshō). The doctrine is elaborated in scriptures like the Tathāgatagarbha Sūtra, the Śrīmālādevī Sūtra, and most importantly the Mahāyāna Mahāparinirvāṇa Sūtra, which contains the foundational claim that all sentient beings have Buddha-nature (一切衆生悉有仏性). Dōgen's radical rereading in the Shōbōgenzō 'Busshō' fascicle proposes that this should be read not as a possession relation ('all beings have Buddha-nature') but as an identity ('all being is Buddha-nature') — a syntactic reanalysis with major doctrinal consequences.

gloss: Buddhahood — the attained condition of being a fully awakened one; the ultimate horizon of Buddhist soteriology, in Mahāyāna understood as the goal of the bodhisattva path
notes: Sanskrit buddhatva is the abstract noun formed from buddha (awakened one); buddha-prāpti is the action of attaining Buddhahood. East Asian Buddhism renders this through the verbal compound 成仏 (jōbutsu), in which the verb of becoming (成) is doing the load-bearing work. The Chan/Zen claim that 成仏 is not the production of something new but the manifestation of what one already is — 見性成仏, seeing one's nature is becoming Buddha — works on the Chinese-Japanese verbal structure in a way that Sanskrit's nominal abstraction does not directly invite. The doctrine of universal Buddhahood (一切衆生悉有仏性) is the necessary condition for this reading.

gloss: the great death — the figural ego-death that must precede genuine awakening; the complete dissolution of the ordinary subject-object structure of consciousness, after which one is 'born into the great life' of awakening
notes: Not a translation from Sanskrit but a Chan Chinese formulation that crystallized in the Linji/Rinzai lineage. The compound is paired structurally with 大悟 (great awakening): the great death is what 大悟 requires as its precondition. The phrase 大死一番、絶後再蘇 (great death one time, then revival after the cutting-off) is a stock Chan formulation describing the sequence: a complete cessation of the discriminating self, followed by a re-emergence into a transformed mode of functioning. The figural language of death is doing precise doctrinal work: it names the kind of transformation awakening requires by analogy to the only experience of total cessation ordinary life provides.

gloss: acquired or initiated enlightenment — awakening as it arrives for a particular practitioner at a particular time, the recognition of what 本覚 (hongaku) names as always-already-the-case
notes: Like 本覚, a Chinese-Japanese doctrinal formation rooted in the Awakening of Faith. The Tendai tradition elaborated 始覚 as the necessary counterpart of 本覚: without 始覚, 本覚 would be a metaphysical claim with no practical purchase; without 本覚, 始覚 would be the production of something new rather than the recognition of what is. The two terms together describe a single relationship — they are not competing accounts of awakening but two perspectives on one event. Dōgen's 修証一等 (oneness of practice and realization) can be read as a way of holding both moments together without privileging either.

gloss: original enlightenment — the doctrine that all sentient beings are intrinsically and originally awakened, with practice and acquired enlightenment (始覚) understood as the recognition rather than the production of what is always already the case
notes: Not directly a translation from Sanskrit but a Chinese-Japanese doctrinal formation rooted in the Awakening of Faith (大乗起信論, Dasheng qixin lun), a sixth-century treatise of disputed authorship that became foundational for East Asian Buddhism. Hongaku thought reaches its most elaborated form in medieval Japanese Tendai, where it develops into a systematic theology of intrinsic awakening that influenced all subsequent Japanese Buddhism — the Pure Land schools, Zen, and Nichiren. Dōgen's relationship to hongaku is complex and central: he interrogates its potential to collapse into a quietism that denies the necessity of practice, while himself developing a position that practice is not separate from the original enlightenment it might seem to seek.

gloss: gradual awakening — the Northern school's doctrine that genuine enlightenment is reached through progressive purification, sustained practice, and the stage-by-stage removal of defilements rather than through a single instantaneous breakthrough
notes: The structural counterpart to 頓悟 (dùnwù). The Platform Sutra polemically associates 漸悟 with Shenxiu and the Northern school, framing it as inferior to Huineng's sudden teaching. Modern scholarship has rehabilitated the so-called Northern school: what was caricatured as a doctrine of grinding incremental purification was in fact a sophisticated practice tradition that did not deny the suddenness of recognition but emphasized the cultivation needed to occasion and stabilize it. The 頓漸 distinction is best read as a difference of pedagogical emphasis within Chan, not as a clean opposition between awakening and non-awakening.

gloss: sudden awakening — the Southern school's doctrine that genuine enlightenment arrives as an instantaneous, structurally complete breakthrough rather than through gradual accumulation of insight
notes: A Chinese Buddhist doctrinal formation with no direct Sanskrit antecedent — the sudden/gradual (頓漸, dùnjiàn) distinction is a controversy internal to Tang-dynasty Chan, not a translation of an Indian debate. The Platform Sutra presents Huineng's teaching as sudden (頓) against Shenxiu's gradual (漸) approach. The doctrinal claim of 頓悟 is not merely that awakening is fast but that it is structurally instantaneous — there is no partial or incomplete awakening that then completes itself over time. The awakening either sees through completely (大悟, great awakening) or has not yet arrived. This raises the pedagogical question of what practice is for if awakening cannot be accumulated: the Chan answer is that practice removes the obscurations preventing recognition of what is already fully present.
